perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, Developer has developed certain computer programs and related documentation more particularly described in Schedule A attached hereto (the "Products") and desires to grant distributor the right to market and distribute the Products in Singapore, Malaysia, Indonesia, Thailand, and Brunei (the "Territory"); and WHEREAS, Distributor is in the business of marketing and distributing computer-related products and desires to have Developer grant to it the right to market and distribute the Products in the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, Developer has developed certain computer programs and related documentation more particularly described in Schedule A attached hereto (the "Software") and desires to grant distributor the right to market and distribute the Products in Singapore, Malaysia, Indonesia, Thailand, and Brunei (the "Territory"); and WHEREAS, Distributor is in the business of marketing and distributing computer-related products and desires to have Developer grant to it the right to market and distribute the Products in the Territory.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Products' to 'Software' in the first definition while keeping the second reference as 'Products' creates an in-text contradiction. Throughout the agreement, it's unclear if 'Products' and 'Software' are the same, impacting the scope of the distribution rights.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Upon the terms and subject to the conditions of this Agreement, Developer hereby grants to Distributor an exclusive, non-transferable fight and license to market and distribute the Products in the Territory. Distributor shall distribute the Products to existing and new customers of Distributor located in the Territory (the "End-Users") who enter into an End-User License Agreement (as hereinafter defined). The Products shall be in executable object code form only and Distributor shall have no fight to the source code of such Products. The Products distributed to End-Users shall be in executable object code form only. Distributor shall not itself, nor allow others to modify, translate, decompile, nor create or attempt to create, by reverse engineering or otherwise, the source code from the object code of the Products supplied hereunder, or adapt the Products in any way or for use to create a derivative work. Should Distributor wish to create such a derivative work, Distributor must first seek and obtain express written permission to do so from the Developer and Developer may withold such permission at its sole discretion. Distributor may not, and may not permit End-Users to, use, reproduce, siblicense, distribute or dispose of the Products, in whole or in part, except as expressly permitted under this Agreement. 1 2
perturbation.justification: The change replaces "Products" with "Software" in the definition section (WHEREAS clause). However, the subsequent reference in the same section, as well as the entire "Contradicted Text" section, continues to use "Products." This creates a strong in-text contradiction because the agreement now uses two different terms, "Software" and "Products," seemingly interchangeably, but without defining them as equivalent. This misalignment makes it unclear whether the distribution rights granted to Distributor apply to "Software," "Products," or both, thus conflicting with the clause that grants the license to distribute "the Products." This uncertainty impacts the enforceability of the agreement and the scope of the distributor's rights, triggering the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Developer retains the right, in its sole discretion, to upgrade or modify the Products from time to time. In addition, upon ninety (90) days prior written notice to Distributor, Developer may add or delete Products from Schedule A. Upon receipt of any such notice of any upgrade or modification, or upon the expiration of the notice period set forth above for additions or deletions to Schedule A, Distributor shall cease to market and distribute earlier versions of the Products and/or Products deleted from Schedule A.
perturbation.changed_text: Developer retains the right, in its sole discretion, to upgrade or modify the Products from time to time. In addition, upon ninety (90) days prior written notice to Distributor, Developer may add or delete applications from Schedule A. Upon receipt of any such notice of any upgrade or modification, or upon the expiration of the notice period set forth above for additions or deletions to Schedule A, Distributor shall cease to market and distribute earlier versions of the Products and/or Products deleted from Schedule A.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Products' to 'applications' when adding or deleting from schedule A creates an inconsistency. Are only "applications" listed in schedule A, and not the "Products"? This is an in-text contradiction, because it becomes unclear if Schedule A lists exclusively "applications" or more generally "Products," potentially affecting the scope of what the distributor is allowed to market.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Upon the terms and subject to the conditions of this Agreement, Developer hereby grants to Distributor an exclusive, non-transferable fight and license to market and distribute the Products in the Territory. Distributor shall distribute the Products to existing and new customers of Distributor located in the Territory (the "End-Users") who enter into an End-User License Agreement (as hereinafter defined). The Products shall be in executable object code form only and Distributor shall have no fight to the source code of such Products. The Products distributed to End-Users shall be in executable object code form only. Distributor shall not itself, nor allow others to modify, translate, decompile, nor create or attempt to create, by reverse engineering or otherwise, the source code from the object code of the Products supplied hereunder, or adapt the Products in any way or for use to create a derivative work. Should Distributor wish to create such a derivative work, Distributor must first seek and obtain express written permission to do so from the Developer and Developer may withold such permission at its sole discretion. Distributor may not, and may not permit End-Users to, use, reproduce, siblicense, distribute or dispose of the Products, in whole or in part, except as expressly permitted under this Agreement. 1 2
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Products' to 'applications' in the context of Schedule A creates a contradiction with the 'Contradicted Text' which grants the distributor rights to market and distribute 'the Products'. If Schedule A only lists 'applications', it's unclear whether the distributor's right extends to all 'Products' or only to those specifically listed as 'applications' in Schedule A. This introduces ambiguity and a potential conflict regarding the scope of the distribution agreement, leading to uncertainty and unenforceability, fitting the definition of Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The terms and conditions of this Agreement shall apply to all Orders submitted to Developer by Distributor. Orders issued by 4 5 Distributor to Developer are solely for the purpose of requesting delivery dates and quantities. All Orders shall be subject to acceptance by Developer; Developer will not unreasonably delay acceptance of any order. Developer shall use reasonable efforts to deliver accepted Orders but shall not be liable for any damages to Distributor or to any third party caused by Developer's delay or error in filling, or failure to fill, any Orders for any reason.
perturbation.changed_text: The terms and conditions of this Agreement shall apply to all Orders submitted to Developer by Distributor. Requests issued by 4 5 Distributor to Developer are solely for the purpose of requesting delivery dates and quantities. All Orders shall be subject to acceptance by Developer; Developer will not unreasonably delay acceptance of any order. Developer shall use reasonable efforts to deliver accepted Orders but shall not be liable for any damages to Distributor or to any third party caused by Developer's delay or error in filling, or failure to fill, any Orders for any reason.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "Orders" to "Requests" in the second sentence introduces ambiguity. This creates in-text contradiction, as it is unclear whether "Orders" and "Requests" are interchangeable, and it is not clear if an order and a request is the same thing.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The terms and conditions of this Agreement shall apply to all Orders submitted to Developer by Distributor. Requests issued by 4 5 Distributor to Developer are solely for the purpose of requesting delivery dates and quantities. All Orders shall be subject to acceptance by Developer; Developer will not unreasonably delay acceptance of any order. Developer shall use reasonable efforts to deliver accepted Orders but shall not be liable for any damages to Distributor or to any third party caused by Developer's delay or error in filling, or failure to fill, any Orders for any reason.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces "Orders" with "Requests" in a sentence that specifies the purpose of these submissions. However, the contract continues to use the term "Orders" elsewhere, including in the sentence immediately following the changed one ("All Orders shall be subject to acceptance by Developer"). This creates ambiguity and an in-text contradiction as to whether "Orders" and "Requests" are interchangeable, potentially leading to disputes over which terms and conditions apply to each type of submission. This falls under the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' because the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.
